AI商业经济算力

Does the AI Business Model Have a Fatal Flaw?

AI的商业模式有致命缺陷吗？算力成本、能耗与收入增长的生死赛跑

Paresh Dave & Anna Tong · Reuters · 2026年4月4日 · 1分钟阅读

中文原文

AI的商业模式有致命缺陷吗？算力成本、能耗与收入增长的生死赛跑

English Original

Does the AI Business Model Have a Fatal Flaw?

By Paresh Dave and Anna Tong | Reuters | April 1, 2026

In the gold rush of artificial intelligence, every company is racing to build the most powerful models. But a growing number of analysts are asking a question that the industry's glossy press releases tend to sidestep: can anyone actually make money at scale?

The Numbers Don't Lie

Training a frontier AI model—the kind that competes with GPT-5 or Gemini 3—costs anywhere from $500 million to over $1 billion per generation. Running that model at commercial scale, serving millions of users, requires data centers drawing power equivalent to small cities. NVIDIA's H100 GPU, the workhorse of the current AI boom, costs around $30,000 to $50,000 per unit. A competitive AI deployment requires thousands of them.

These are not the economics of traditional software. A software product, once built, can be copied for near-zero marginal cost. AI is different: every query costs compute, every model update costs energy, and every user interaction generates real-time expenses that must be covered by revenue.

The Revenue Problem

So where does the money come from? The dominant business models in AI today are:

API access: Charging per-token or per-query fees for access to models. This is how OpenAI, Anthropic, and Google generate most of their current revenue.Subscription products: ChatGPT Plus, Claude Pro, Gemini Advanced—the consumer subscription layer.Enterprise software: Integrating AI capabilities into existing enterprise workflows and charging accordingly.

The problem is that all three models face structural pressure. API pricing has collapsed by roughly 99% over the past three years as competition intensified and inference costs dropped. Consumer subscriptions have reached saturation among early adopters, and enterprise deals take months to close and are subject to long procurement cycles.

Microsoft's $10 Billion Bet

Microsoft's announcement that it will invest $10 billion in Japanese AI infrastructure between 2026 and 2029 is typical of the current investment thesis: spend massively now, figure out the returns later. Microsoft's strategy is to embed AI capabilities across its entire product portfolio—Office, Azure, Teams—and extract rents from the resulting productivity gains.

Whether this calculus works depends on a central question: does AI actually make workers meaningfully more productive, and if so, will employers pay for it?

The evidence so far is mixed. Studies of AI-assisted coding show productivity gains of 30-50% for individual developers. Studies of AI in customer service show more modest gains, offset by the costs of retraining and integration. In knowledge work more broadly—the promised land of AI productivity—the data is still thin.

The Energy Ceiling

There is a physical constraint that may prove more binding than any market or regulatory force: energy.

A typical data center drawing 100 megawatts of power—enough for 100,000 homes—can support perhaps 10,000 to 20,000 AI inference servers, depending on utilization. Training runs are even more demanding: a single training run for a frontier model can consume as much electricity as a small town uses in a year.

The major cloud providers—Microsoft, Google, Amazon—are all constrained by the availability of electrical power in the regions where they want to build data centers. Microsoft has signed power purchase agreements with nuclear and renewable energy providers that would have been unimaginable for a software company a decade ago.

The Consolidation Question

If the economics of frontier AI are this challenging, why are so many companies still chasing the frontier? The answer is partly competitive positioning and partly the structure of risk capital. Venture funds and tech giants can afford to lose billions on AI bets because the alternative—falling behind in what might be the most transformative technology in a generation—is seen as worse.

The likely outcome is consolidation. As training costs rise and API prices fall, only a handful of companies will be able to afford frontier model development. Everyone else will either build on top of existing models, specialize in particular domains or modalities, or exit the market.

This is already happening. Over the past 18 months, at least a dozen well-funded AI startups have either shut down or pivoted to consulting, having concluded that the model development race was not survivable.

The Open Source Variable

The open-source AI movement adds another layer of uncertainty. Meta's Llama series, DeepSeek's open releases, and a growing ecosystem of community-developed models have dramatically lowered the cost of accessing capable AI.

When a frontier capability becomes freely available open-source, it becomes very difficult to charge premium prices for it. The business model implications of open-source AI are not yet fully clear—but they are likely to accelerate the consolidation and price pressure already reshaping the industry.

The Bottom Line

The AI industry is in a period of transition from a growth-at-all-costs mentality to something more resembling normal business economics. This transition will be painful for some players and clarifying for others.

The companies that survive the next several years will be those that find genuine product-market fit—not just demos that impress investors, but tools that users pay for at prices that cover their costs. Everything else is noise.

Reuters. Used with permission.
